name: Ramanuja
dates_notes: Traditional 120-year lifespan suspect; academic consensus narrows the dates.

lineage_polemical.note: Rāmānuja famously broke from his bhedābhedin teacher Yādava-Prakāśa; Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself partly against this earlier position.
Rāmānuja's Śrī-Bhāṣya is the principal Viśiṣṭādvaitin polemic against Advaitic māyā via the sapta-vidhā-anupapatti.

core_thesis: Rāmānuja gives Viśiṣṭādvaita its school-defining form. Brahman — Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī — is the sole independent reality, but its unity is *viśiṣṭa* (qualified), not undifferentiated. The infinite plurality of conscious selves (*cit*) and non-conscious matter (*acit*) constitutes Brahman's body (*śarīra*); Brahman is their *śarīrin* and *antaryāmin* (inner controller). *Cit* and *acit* are really distinct from each other and from Brahman, yet inseparable (*apṛthak-siddha*) — they cannot exist or be conceived apart from him. There is therefore no unqualified *nirviśeṣa* Brahman; *neti neti* negates limiting attributes, not all distinction. Against Śaṅkara's *māyāvāda* the *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §76 ff. unfolds the *sapta-vidhānupapatti* — seven distinct destructive arguments against Advaitin *avidyā*. Two examples make the structure visible. (i) *Āśrayānupapatti*: locate avidyā's substrate. If in Brahman, Brahman ceases to be self-luminous *cit*; if in the *jīva*, the *jīva* is itself a product of *avidyā* and the explanation circles. (ii) *Tirodhānānupapatti*: avidyā is supposed to conceal Brahman; but Brahman is by definition *svayam-prakāśa*, so concealment of Brahman is concealment of light by darkness as such — an event with no possible mechanism. The remaining five (against avidyā's nature, indeterminability, *pramāṇa*, *nivartaka*, and cessation) operate by the same destructive technique: take the Advaitin's own characterisation, run it through its consequences, exhibit the contradiction. The constructive position then follows. The *jīva* is an atomic, eternal *jñātṛ* (knower) with *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna* — substance-awareness bearing real attributes. *Tat tvam asi* is read on the body-soul model of *sāmānādhikaraṇya*: *tat* and *tvam* co-predicate one substratum because Brahman is the inner self of the *jīva*, not because they are numerically identical. *Mokṣa* is the *jīva*'s perfected *kaiṅkarya* (beatific service) to Nārāyaṇa, not the dissolution of personhood. *Bhakti-yoga* — meditative devotion as enjoined *upāsanā* — is the constitutive form of *mokṣa*; *prapatti* (utter self-surrender) is a parallel path, open especially to those incapable of sustained *bhakti-yoga*.

engaged_works.title: Shri-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.
engaged_works.summary: The school-defining commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's *Brahma-Sūtra*, written (per tradition) on the basis of Bodhāyana's *Vṛtti* and the Drāviḍa lineage. The *Mahā-Siddhānta* on *sūtra* 1.1.1 (*athāto brahma-jijñāsā*) opens with the *Mahā-Pūrvapakṣa* — an unusually fair and full statement of the Advaitic position — followed by the *sapta-vidhā-anupapatti*, the seven structural untenabilities of *avidyā*: where it resides, what it conceals (since Brahman is self-luminous), what its nature is, why it is indeterminable, what its *pramāṇa* could be, what removes it, and what its cessation amounts to. The *Śrī-Bhāṣya* then constructs the positive Viśiṣṭādvaita reading of the *sūtra*-s *sūtra* by *sūtra*: *samanvaya* establishes that all *śruti*-passages, including the *mahā-vākya* *tat tvam asi*, predicate Brahman *as qualified by* the world of *cit* and *acit* (the *sāmānādhikaraṇya* of body-soul reference), not as identical with the *jīva* in any unqualified sense. The work matters because it converts Yāmuna's positions into a complete, *sūtra*-anchored alternative to Śaṅkara within the same shared exegetical genre.

engaged_works.title: Vedartha-Samgraha
engaged_works.summary: Independent *prakaraṇa* synthesising the Viśiṣṭādvaita reading of the *Upaniṣad*-corpus prior to and apart from the *sūtra*-frame. Its core target is the Advaitic exegesis of the *mahā-vākya* *tat tvam asi*: Rāmānuja shows that *tat* and *tvam*, being terms with determinate references (Brahman-as-qualified-by-the-world and the *jīva*-as-embodied-self respectively), can stand in *sāmānādhikaraṇya* — co-predication of one substratum — only on the body-soul model, where Brahman is *antaryāmin* of the self that *tvam* denotes. Hence the great-sentence teaches non-difference *as* the inseparability (*apṛthak-siddhi*) of self and inner-self, not numerical identity. The *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* lays out the *sapta-vidhā-anupapatti* against *avidyā* and develops the doctrine of *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna* (substance-awareness bearing real attributes). It is also where Rāmānuja most explicitly engages and refutes Yādava-Prakāśa's *bhedābheda* and Bhāskara's *aupādhika* version of identity-difference. The work matters as the most accessible single statement of the school's positive metaphysics and its constructive scriptural hermeneutics.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Dipa
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compact commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.
engaged_works.summary: A medium-length commentary on the *Brahma-Sūtra*, intended as a more compact exposition of the same Viśiṣṭādvaita reading developed in the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*. The *Vedānta-Dīpa* preserves the structural moves — *samanvaya* of *śruti* on *Brahman saviśeṣa*, the *śarīra-śarīrin* relation as the key to *sāmānādhikaraṇya*, the rejection of *nirviśeṣa* Brahman and *avidyā* — but trims the polemical *pūrvapakṣa* sections and the long Bhāskara-Yādava-Prakāśa engagements. It functions in the lineage as the bridge between the encyclopaedic *Śrī-Bhāṣya* and the still shorter *Vedānta-Sāra*, and was meant to serve advanced students who already grasped the framework. The work matters because Rāmānuja's willingness to recapitulate the entire *sūtra*-pāṭha three times at three lengths (*Bhāṣya*, *Dīpa*, *Sāra*) testifies to his pedagogical seriousness about transmission: the *Vedānta-Dīpa* is what the school used to *teach* the system once the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* had established it.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Sara
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored shorter commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.
engaged_works.summary: The shortest of Rāmānuja's three commentaries on the *Brahma-Sūtra* — a pithy *sūtra*-by-*sūtra* gloss giving only the bare Viśiṣṭādvaita reading, without extended *pūrvapakṣa* or polemical apparatus. Designed for memorisation and oral transmission, the *Vedānta-Sāra* serves as the entry-text by which a student internalises the school's *adhikaraṇa*-divisions and the determinate Viśiṣṭādvaita gloss of each *sūtra* before moving up to the *Dīpa* and finally the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*. Its brevity is itself a doctrinal statement: the school holds that the Viśiṣṭādvaita reading of the *sūtra*-s is the obvious one once the body-soul reading of *sāmānādhikaraṇya* is grasped, and so the basic gloss can be transmitted in compressed form. The work matters as the canonical pedagogical entry-point to Rāmānuja's *sūtra*-exegesis and as evidence of his deliberate structuring of the school's curriculum at three escalating levels of detail.

engaged_works.title: Gita-Bhashya
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on the *Bhagavad-Gītā* organising the eighteen chapters into three *ṣaṭka*-s on the three *yoga*-s — *karma*, *jñāna*, *bhakti* — with *bhakti-yoga* as the culminating and highest. Against the Advaitic gloss that reads the *Gītā* as teaching *jñāna* to which devotion is an upāya, Rāmānuja argues that *bhakti-yoga* is itself the operative form of mature *upāsanā*: meditative devotion to Kṛṣṇa-as-Para-Brahman that, once intensified to the perspicuity of direct presence (*sākṣātkāra*), *is* the liberating cognition. The decisive moment is the gloss on *Gītā* 18.66 (*sarva-dharmān parityajya …*), the *carama-śloka*, which Rāmānuja reads as enjoining *prapatti* — utter self-surrender — as a parallel and equally efficacious path for those incapable of sustained *bhakti-yoga*. The work matters because it grounds the school's two-path soteriology in the *Gītā* itself and provides the definitive Vaiṣṇava counter-reading of every passage Śaṅkara had taken to teach world-renouncing monism.

engaged_works.title: Gadya-Trayam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed and accepted in tradition; modern scholarship occasionally questions individual passages.
engaged_works.summary: Three short Sanskrit prose-hymns — *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya*, *Śrīraṅga-Gadya*, *Vaikuṇṭha-Gadya* — traditionally said to have been composed at Śrīraṅgam on the Pañcamī of the bright fortnight of Phālguna, in which Rāmānuja enacts his own *prapatti* to Śrī-Raṅganātha and Śrī. The *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* is the locus classicus for the school's articulation of *prapatti* as self-surrender qualified by the six *aṅga*-s (*ānukūlya-saṅkalpa*, *prātikūlya-varjana*, *rakṣiṣyatīti viśvāsa*, *goptṛtva-varaṇa*, *ātma-nikṣepa*, *kārpaṇya*) and as performed in the presence of Śrī as *puruṣakāra*. The *Śrīraṅga-Gadya* and *Vaikuṇṭha-Gadya* extend the act into ongoing kaiṅkarya and contemplation of the Lord's *parama-pada*. The texts matter doctrinally as the bridge between Rāmānuja the systematician and the school's lived devotional practice; the Tenkalai-Vaḍakalai split later turns substantially on how the *aṅga*-s of *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* are interpreted — as conditions the surrendered self must enact (Vaḍakalai) or as descriptions of what surrender already is (Tenkalai).

engaged_works.title: Nitya-Grantha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed manual of daily worship.
engaged_works.summary: Short Sanskrit manual prescribing the daily *ārādhana* of Nārāyaṇa for the qualified Śrīvaiṣṇava — covering the morning *snāna*, *sandhyā*, *pūjā*, the five-fold *pañca-kāla* division of the day into Vaiṣṇava observances, and the *mantra*-recitation appropriate to each. The *Nitya-Grantha* operationalises the contemplative life implied by Rāmānuja's *bhakti-yoga*: meditative remembrance of Bhagavān is to be sustained not as an episodic spiritual exercise but as the form of every day's structure. It is also where Rāmānuja codifies the Pāñcarātra-derived *mantra*-system used in Śrīvaiṣṇava temple and household worship, integrating it with smārta *nitya-karma*. The work matters because it shows that for Rāmānuja Viśiṣṭādvaita is not merely a philosophical position but a *vidhi* — an enjoined form of life — and because it is the textual basis for the daily liturgy still in use in the Śrīvaiṣṇava temple system. The traditional ascription is secure; modern scholarship occasionally raises minor source-critical questions but accepts core authorship.
